refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall maintain teleoperation communication packet loss at or below the customer-defined threshold (acceptance criterion: ≤1% under agreed network conditions) to ensure safe and effective remote equipment operation.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-009 addresses a critical quality-of-service requirement for teleoperation. Packet loss directly impacts operator situational awareness, control responsiveness, and safety. Refining the FR to explicitly reference the acceptance criterion (≤1%) and network conditions scope clarifies the verification boundary and prevents ambiguity in acceptance testing.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Conduct packet loss measurement under representative network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles agreed with customer). Measure end-to-end packet loss across the teleoperation communication path (operator input → vehicle → operator feedback). Acceptance: measured packet loss ≤ customer-defined limit across 95th percentile of test scenarios.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator control commands to the vehicle with minimal loss.
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle telemetry and sensor data to the operator with minimal loss.
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss threshold breach) to safety services.
FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement adaptive quality-of-service mechanisms to maintain packet loss within acceptable limits under varying network conditions.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement redundant communication channels (dual network paths) with automatic failover to reduce single-point-of-failure packet loss impact.
DP-B: Deploy adaptive bitrate encoding and packet prioritization (QoS) to minimize loss under congestion; pair with forward error correction (FEC) for critical control/safety messages.
DP-C: Establish dedicated, isolated network infrastructure (private 5G or licensed spectrum) to eliminate contention and guarantee packet loss performance.
DP-D: Implement hybrid approach combining redundant channels (DP-A), QoS/FEC (DP-B), and network monitoring with dynamic rerouting to optimize packet loss across multiple failure modes.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Implement a hybrid teleoperation communication architecture combining (1) redundant communication channels with automatic failover, (2) adaptive bitrate encoding, packet prioritization (QoS), and forward error correction (FEC) for critical messages, and (3) real-time network monitoring with dynamic rerouting to maintain packet loss ≤ customer threshold under agreed network conditions.

potential_sources: DP-D couples teleoperation control logic (Tasking interface) with network infrastructure (Network Link interface) and safety monitoring (SafetyAlert interface). This is acceptable because packet loss is inherently a network-quality concern that must integrate across these domains.
DP-D does not create unintended coupling to vehicle propulsion, hydraulics, or mechanical systems; it is logically isolated to the communication layer.
Redundancy and FEC add computational overhead; coupling to vehicle compute resources (CPU, memory) is expected and manageable via resource allocation in the vehicle module.
coupling_check.notes: DP-D is a systems-level solution that necessarily spans multiple functional domains (teleoperation, safety, network). This is appropriate for a quality-of-service requirement. No problematic decoupling violations detected.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Customer-defined packet loss threshold and network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference) are formally documented and agreed before detailed design.
ASSUMPTION: Vehicle and operator infrastructure support dual communication channels (e.g., LTE + WiFi, or dual LTE carriers).
ASSUMPTION: FEC overhead (increased payload size) is acceptable within network bandwidth constraints.

RISK: If network conditions are more severe than assumed (e.g., intermittent blackouts >1 second), redundancy and FEC alone may be insufficient; fallback to manual operation or geofenced autonomy may be required.
RISK: Adaptive bitrate encoding may degrade operator situational awareness (lower video/sensor fidelity) under poor network conditions; safety impact must be assessed.
RISK: Dynamic rerouting logic adds complexity and potential for latency spikes; real-time control responsiveness must be validated.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-D to explicitly specify (1) the redundancy strategy (e.g., active-active vs. active-passive), (2) FEC codec selection and overhead budget, (3) QoS priority levels for different message types (safety-critical vs. informational), and (4) fallback behavior when packet loss exceeds threshold despite mitigation. This will reduce ambiguity in implementation and enable clearer IM assignment.

im_boundary.name: Teleoperation
im_boundary.scope: The Teleoperation module owns the end-to-end communication quality assurance for remote equipment operation, including redundant channel management, adaptive encoding, FEC, QoS prioritization, network monitoring, and dynamic rerouting logic. It interfaces with the Vehicle module (to access compute resources and control/telemetry data), Safety Services (to report communication quality events), and external Network Link infrastructure.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator control commands (e.g., movement, tool actuation) received from the operator console; Teleoperation module encodes, prioritizes, and transmits via redundant channels.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle sensor data (position, orientation, load, hydraulic pressure, etc.) collected from Vehicle module; Teleoperation module encodes, prioritizes, and transmits to operator console via redundant channels.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Communication quality degradation alerts (packet loss threshold breach, channel failure, latency spike) transmitted to Safety Services for operator notification and potential intervention (e.g., geofenced autonomy, manual takeover).

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Dual communication channels (e.g., LTE + WiFi, or dual LTE carriers) managed by Teleoperation module; includes channel selection, failover logic, and real-time quality monitoring.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system container. Assigning DP-D (packet loss mitigation) to NGHE Solution would diffuse responsibility across all sub-modules and prevent clear ownership of communication quality. Not recommended as primary IM boundary.

Vehicle module owns propulsion, hydraulics, sensors, and onboard compute. While the vehicle hosts communication hardware (modem, antenna), the packet loss mitigation logic (redundancy, FEC, QoS, rerouting) is not inherently a vehicle concern; it is a teleoperation system concern. Assigning DP-D to Vehicle would couple communication quality logic to vehicle mechanics, reducing cohesion. Partial containment only for hardware resources.

Teleoperation module is the natural owner of end-to-end communication quality. It directly manages redundant channels, adaptive encoding, FEC, QoS, network monitoring, and rerouting. All DP-D logic is cohesive within this module. Interfaces with Vehicle (for data), Safety Services (for alerts), and Network Link (for channels) are well-defined and minimal. Highly recommended.

Safety Services owns hazard detection, risk assessment, and safety interventions (e.g., emergency stop, geofenced autonomy). While Safety Services consumes SafetyAlert events from Teleoperation, it does not own the communication quality logic itself. Assigning DP-D to Safety Services would blur the boundary between communication quality and safety response. Not recommended.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: The Teleoperation module is the optimal IM boundary for DP-D because (1) it owns the end-to-end teleoperation communication path, (2) redundancy, FEC, QoS, and rerouting logic are cohesive within this module, (3) external interfaces (Tasking, Telemetry, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are minimal and well-defined, and (4) it avoids diffusion of communication quality responsibility across Vehicle or Safety Services modules.
containment_risks: RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle module to provide telemetry data and accept control commands. If Vehicle module has latency or data loss issues upstream, Teleoperation's mitigation may be insufficient. Mitigation: Define clear SLAs for Vehicle-to-Teleoperation data handoff (e.g., <50ms latency, 100% availability).

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Network Link infrastructure (dual channels, failover). If Network Link is not reliably available or failover is slow (>100ms), packet loss may exceed threshold. Mitigation: Validate Network Link performance in customer's agreed network conditions before deployment.

RISK: Adaptive bitrate encoding and FEC add computational overhead. If Vehicle compute resources are constrained, Teleoperation may not be able to implement full DP-D. Mitigation: Allocate sufficient CPU/memory in Vehicle module for Teleoperation's encoding/FEC tasks; consider offloading to edge compute if available.

RISK: SafetyAlert interface requires Teleoperation to detect and report communication quality events. If detection logic is delayed or inaccurate, Safety Services may not respond in time. Mitigation: Define clear thresholds and latency budgets for SafetyAlert generation (e.g., <500ms from packet loss detection to alert transmission).
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Clarify the boundary between Teleoperation and Vehicle modules regarding compute resource allocation for FEC/encoding. Define explicit SLAs for Vehicle-to-Teleoperation data handoff (latency, availability). Specify the failover latency budget for Network Link redundancy to ensure end-to-end packet loss target is achievable. Document the SafetyAlert generation logic and latency budget to ensure Safety Services can respond appropriately.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid redundancy, QoS, FEC, monitoring, rerouting) directly addresses the refined FR (maintain packet loss ≤ customer threshold under agreed network conditions). The DP is comprehensive, covering multiple failure modes (channel loss, congestion, latency spikes) and includes both preventive (redundancy, FEC) and reactive (monitoring, rerouting) mechanisms. The DP is implementable and verifiable. No decoupling violations detected.

diffusion_risks: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle module for compute resources (CPU, memory for FEC/encoding). If Vehicle module is resource-constrained, Teleoperation's DP may be compromised. Mitigation: Allocate sufficient resources in Vehicle module design; consider edge compute offloading if needed.

Teleoperation module depends on Network Link infrastructure for dual channels and failover. If Network Link is not reliably available, DP-D cannot be fully realized. Mitigation: Validate Network Link performance in customer's agreed network conditions; define SLAs for failover latency.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: Teleoperation module has high containment (0.95) and low diffusion (0.05) for DP-D. All core logic (redundancy, FEC, QoS, monitoring, rerouting) is cohesive within Teleoperation. External dependencies (Vehicle compute, Network Link) are well-defined and manageable via interface SLAs. No problematic diffusion detected.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: Verification of FR-NGHE-009 is feasible via end-to-end packet loss measurement under customer-agreed network conditions. Teleoperation module can be instrumented to measure and log packet loss across redundant channels, FEC recovery, and QoS prioritization. Acceptance testing can validate that measured packet loss ≤ customer threshold across 95th percentile of test scenarios. Clear acceptance criteria and measurement methodology are required.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: The allocation of FR-NGHE-009 to Teleoperation module is clear. Teleoperation owns the end-to-end communication quality assurance. Sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) can be further decomposed and allocated to specific Teleoperation sub-components (e.g., redundancy manager, FEC encoder, QoS scheduler, network monitor). No ambiguity in allocation.

recommended_revisions: Formalize the customer-defined packet loss threshold and agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles) in a requirements specification document. This is critical for verification and acceptance testing.
Define explicit SLAs for Vehicle-to-Teleoperation data handoff (latency, availability) and Network Link failover latency to ensure end-to-end packet loss target is achievable.
Specify the SafetyAlert generation logic and latency budget (e.g., <500ms from packet loss detection to alert transmission) to ensure Safety Services can respond appropriately.
Allocate sufficient compute resources in Vehicle module for Teleoperation's FEC/encoding tasks; document resource requirements and constraints.
Plan for detailed decomposition of sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) in the next level to address specific failure modes (channel loss, congestion, latency spikes) and mitigation strategies.

decision.justification: The refined FR-NGHE-009 is well-defined, the selected DP-D is comprehensive and implementable, and the assigned IM (Teleoperation module) has high containment and clear interfaces. All three gates (FR-DP, DP-IM, FR-IM) pass. However, DP-D is complex and spans multiple sub-concerns (redundancy, FEC, QoS, monitoring, rerouting). To ensure detailed design clarity and risk mitigation, the next level should decompose the four sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) into more granular functional requirements and design parameters. This will enable clearer ownership, testability, and risk management for each mitigation strategy.
decision.justification_detail: DP-D is a systems-level solution that combines multiple technologies (redundancy, FEC, QoS, dynamic rerouting). While the high-level logic is sound, the detailed design of each component (e.g., FEC codec selection, QoS priority levels, rerouting algorithm) requires further decomposition. Sub-FRs provide a natural decomposition path. Proceeding to the next level will reduce implementation risk and improve verification clarity.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-009.1: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit operator control commands to the vehicle with minimal loss (target: <0.5% loss on control channel).
FR-NGHE-009.2: The teleoperation system shall reliably transmit vehicle telemetry and sensor data to the operator with minimal loss (target: <1% loss on telemetry channel).
FR-NGHE-009.3: The teleoperation system shall detect and report communication quality degradation (packet loss threshold breach, latency spike, channel failure) to safety services within 500ms.

FR-NGHE-009.4: The teleoperation system shall implement adaptive quality-of-service mechanisms (bitrate scaling, FEC, packet prioritization) to maintain packet loss within acceptable limits under varying network conditions (latency 0-500ms, bandwidth 1-10 Mbps, loss 0-5%).

assumptions: Customer-defined packet loss threshold (≤1%) and agreed network conditions (latency, bandwidth, interference) are formally documented and stable.
Vehicle module provides reliable telemetry data and accepts control commands with <50ms latency and 100% availability.
Network Link infrastructure supports dual communication channels (e.g., LTE + WiFi) with automatic failover capability.
Vehicle compute resources are sufficient to support FEC encoding/decoding and QoS scheduling overhead.
Safety Services module is available to receive and act on SafetyAlert events from Teleoperation module.
Operator console (tele-operator interface) is capable of receiving and processing adaptive bitrate video/sensor streams.
open_risks: RISK: If customer-agreed network conditions are more severe than assumed (e.g., intermittent blackouts >1 second), redundancy and FEC alone may be insufficient. Fallback to manual operation or geofenced autonomy may be required. MITIGATION: Conduct network characterization study in customer's actual deployment environment before detailed design.

RISK: Adaptive bitrate encoding may degrade operator situational awareness under poor network conditions, impacting safety. MITIGATION: Define minimum acceptable video/sensor fidelity levels; validate operator performance under degraded conditions.

RISK: Dynamic rerouting logic adds complexity and potential for latency spikes, impacting real-time control responsiveness. MITIGATION: Validate rerouting latency budget (<100ms) in simulation and field testing.

RISK: Teleoperation module depends on Vehicle compute resources for FEC/encoding. If Vehicle is resource-constrained, DP-D may be compromised. MITIGATION: Allocate sufficient resources in Vehicle module design; consider edge compute offloading.

RISK: SafetyAlert generation logic may be delayed or inaccurate, preventing timely Safety Services response. MITIGATION: Define clear thresholds and latency budgets for SafetyAlert generation (<500ms).
evidence_needed: Formal requirements specification documenting customer-defined packet loss threshold and agreed network conditions.
Network characterization study in customer's actual deployment environment (latency, bandwidth, interference profiles).
Vehicle module compute resource allocation plan (CPU, memory for FEC/encoding).
Network Link failover latency measurement and validation (target: <100ms).
SafetyAlert generation logic specification and latency budget validation.
Operator performance validation under adaptive bitrate conditions (video/sensor fidelity vs. situational awareness).
End-to-end packet loss measurement and acceptance testing results (target: ≤1% under agreed network conditions).
Detailed design and implementation of sub-FRs (FR-NGHE-009.1 through 009.4) in the next decomposition level.
